title: Emotion Analysis Worksheet
description: Use this worksheet to understand your emotions more deeply. Accurate emotion labeling is a core DBT skill — when you can name exactly what you feel and understand why, you gain access to more effective responses. This worksheet walks you through identifying, labeling, understanding, and responding to a strong emotion.
instructions: When you experience a strong emotion (positive or negative), work through each step below. The goal is not to eliminate the emotion but to understand it clearly enough to respond wisely rather than reactively.

fields.label: 1. What happened? (Facts only — who, what, when, where)
fields.placeholder: e.g., My friend cancelled our plans 30 minutes before we were supposed to meet, saying something came up

fields.label: 2. What emotion(s) did you feel? (Name them as specifically as possible)

options.label: Anger — feeling wronged, violated, or blocked
Sadness — feeling loss, disconnection, or disappointment
Fear — sensing threat, danger, or vulnerability
Shame — feeling fundamentally flawed or exposed
Guilt — feeling you did something wrong or harmful
Jealousy / Envy — feeling threatened by loss or comparison
Disgust — feeling revulsion or wanting to push away
Love / Tenderness — feeling connection or care
Joy — feeling pleasure, delight, or satisfaction

fields.label: 3. How intense was the emotion? (0 = barely noticeable, 100 = most intense ever)
4. Where did you feel it in your body? What physical sensations did you notice?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Tightness in chest, heat in face, clenched jaw, stomach dropping, racing heart, heaviness in limbs

fields.label: 5. What was the thought or interpretation that fueled the emotion?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'They don't care about me', 'I'm not important to anyone', 'They're lying about why they cancelled'

fields.label: 6. What is this emotion telling you? (What does it want you to do?)
fields.placeholder: e.g., Anger wants me to confront them or punish them. Sadness wants me to withdraw. Fear wants me to avoid making plans again.

fields.label: 7. Check the facts: Is the emotion fitting the facts of the situation, or is it based on an interpretation?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Fact: they cancelled. Interpretation: they don't care. Alternative: they may have a real conflict. One cancellation doesn't mean they don't value me.

fields.label: 8. Wise Mind response: What is the most effective thing to do right now?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Text them 'No worries, hope everything's okay' (opposite action to attacking). Plan something enjoyable for myself tonight. Revisit this if it becomes a pattern.

fields.label: 9. How intense is the emotion now? (0 = barely noticeable, 100 = most intense ever)

distortionsList.name: Emotion Mind Dominance
distortionsList.description: Treating feelings as facts. 'I feel rejected, so I AM rejected.' 'I feel like a failure, so I AM a failure.' Emotions are real but they're not always accurate reflections of reality.

distortionsList.name: Black-and-White Thinking
distortionsList.description: Seeing situations in extremes: 'They always do this' or 'Nobody cares about me.' Reality usually lives in the gray area between extremes.

distortionsList.name: Catastrophizing
distortionsList.description: Jumping to the worst possible outcome. 'This means our friendship is over.' Check: what is the most likely outcome, not the worst one?

distortionsList.name: Mind-Reading
distortionsList.description: Assuming you know what others think or feel without checking. 'They cancelled because they find me boring.' Ask: do I actually have evidence for this?

distortionsList.name: Should Statements
distortionsList.description: Rigid rules about how things must be. 'They should have given me more notice.' 'I shouldn't feel this way.' Replace 'should' with 'I'd prefer' to reduce intensity.

distortionsList.name: Emotional Reasoning
distortionsList.description: Using your emotional state as evidence. 'I feel anxious, so something bad must be about to happen.' 'I feel worthless, so I must be worthless.' Emotions are data, not verdicts.

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational self-reflection, not a substitute for professional DBT therapy. If you are experiencing overwhelming emotions, urges to self-harm, or suicidal thoughts, please reach out: call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline), text HOME to 741741 (Crisis Text Line), or call 911 for emergencies.
